title: Technique: Become Your Own Advocate
description: This technique offers a fresh way to overcome self-criticism by stepping into the role of your inner advocate. Using a courtroom analogy, you’ll learn to notice accusatory thoughts, challenge them, and defend yourself with arguments grounded in facts and good faith. The method helps cultivate a more supportive, rational, and respectful attitude toward yourself.

steps: Technique: Become Your Own Advocate
The following technique helps you work with self-criticism by inviting you to become your own advocate. It may sound unusual, but it’s surprisingly effective.
To weaken the impact of critical thoughts, it’s important not only to notice them but also to counter them with confidence.
Imagine you’re in a courtroom. Your inner critical thoughts play the role of the prosecutor, assigning labels like “failure,” “weak,” or “to blame for everything.”
Now you take on the role of the defense. Your task is to dispute the charges and show that they’re unfounded and distorted.
<q data-author="Elizabeth Gilbert">Be the advocate of your inner world, not the judge of your own story.</q>
Picture yourself as a competent attorney. You won’t stop at “I’m a good person”—you’ll bring real facts, logical arguments, and sound explanations.
This is an inner dialogue grounded in evidence, not emotion.
Below is an example of how this can look in practice:
<dialog data-psy >Imagine you often criticize yourself, calling yourself weak and a failure. Now try stepping into the role of the defense—even if it feels difficult at first.</dialog>
<dialog data-psy >The goal is to defend yourself honestly, thoughtfully, and respectfully, as if you were defending someone you care about.</dialog>
<dialog  data-man>A psychologist suggests an exercise:</dialog>
<dialog data-psy >All right, let’s try a role-play. I’ll be the prosecutor and you’ll be the defense attorney.</dialog>
<dialog  data-man>Ready? Let’s begin.</dialog>
<dialog data-psy >(Prosecutor) Paul is weak and a failure; nothing he does works out.</dialog>
<dialog  data-man>(Defense) That’s not accurate. Paul earned a degree, has achieved career milestones, and is surrounded by people who value him.</dialog>
<dialog data-psy >(Prosecutor) But he makes mistakes and doesn’t always handle things well—so he’s still a failure.</dialog>
<dialog  data-man>(Defense) Mistakes are part of every human life. They don’t define a person or prove incompetence.</dialog>
<dialog data-psy >(Prosecutor) Fine. But he’s weak.</dialog>
<dialog  data-man>(Defense) Do you have evidence? Paul works hard, makes decisions, and handles difficulties. His efforts show the opposite.</dialog>
<dialog data-psy >(Prosecutor) He could try harder.</dialog>
<dialog  data-man>(Defense) If he truly lacked diligence, he wouldn’t be valued at work or considered for promotion. The claim is unfounded.</dialog>
This technique works because many people find it easier to defend someone else than themselves. Here, you learn to become your own ally.
<important>As the defense, you check facts, demand evidence, and don’t let baseless criticism pass as truth.</important>
When negative thoughts arise, ask: “How would I defend someone I want to support?”
Approach it professionally: analyze, test the logic, and look for facts.
Here are helpful questions:
• What is the charge? What exactly are my thoughts “accusing” me of?
• Is there real evidence, or is this emotion talking?
• What facts speak in my favor?
• Can the situation be explained differently—in a kinder, fairer way?
• What would a reasonable person do in my place?
• Are there external circumstances that influenced what happened?
• What can I say in my defense? Which qualities and achievements can I present as arguments?
